author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Homily 1

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Homilies of St. John Chrysostom,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: archbishop of constantinople,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: gospel according to st. matthew.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It were indeed meet for us not at all to requirethe aid of the written Word, but to exhibit a life so pure, that the grace of the Spirit should be instead of books to our souls, and that as these are inscribed with ink, even so should our hearts be with the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But, since we have utterly put away from us this grace, come, let us at any rate embrace the second best course.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that the former was better, God hath made manifest,both by His words, and by His doings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since unto Noah, and unto Abraham, and unto his offspring, and unto Job, and unto Moses too, He discoursed not by writings, but Himself by Himself, finding their mind pure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But after the whole people of the Hebrews had fallen into the very pit of wickedness, then and thereafter was a written word, and tables, and the admonition which is given by these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this one may perceive was the case, not of the saints in the Old Testament only, but also of those in the New.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither to the apostles did God give anything in writing, but instead of written words He promised that He would give them the grace of the Spirit: for “He,” saith our Lord, “shall bring all things to your remembrance.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that thou mayest learn that this was far better, hear what He saith by the Prophet: “I will make a new covenant with you, putting my laws into their mind, and in their heart I will write them,” and, “they shall be all taught of God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Paul too, pointing out the same superiority, said, that they had received a law “not in tables of stone, but in fleshy tables of the heart.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But since in process of time they made shipwreck, some with regard to doctrines, others as to life and manners, there was again need that they should be put in remembrance by the written word.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Reflect then how great an evil it is for us, who ought to live so purely as not even to need written words, but to yield up our hearts, as books, to the Spirit; now that we have lost that honor, and are come to have need of these, to fail again in duly employing even this second remedy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if it be a blame to stand in need of written words, and not to have brought down on ourselves the grace of the Spirit; consider how heavy the charge of not choosing to profit even after this assistance, but rather treating what is written with neglect, as if it were cast forth without purpose, and at random, and so bringing down upon ourselves our punishment with increase.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But that no such effect may ensue, let us give strict heed unto the things that are written; and let us learn how the Old Law was given on the one hand, how on the other the New Covenant.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then was that law given in time past, and when, and where?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After the destruction of the Egyptians, in the wilderness, on Mount Sinai, when smoke and fire were rising up out of the mountain, a trumpet sounding, thunders and lightnings, and Moses entering into the very depth of the cloud.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But in the new covenant not so,—neither in a wilderness, nor in a mountain, nor with smoke and darkness and cloud and tempest; but at the beginning of the day, in a house, while all were sitting together, with great quietness, all took place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to those, being more unreasonable, and hard to guide, there was need of outward pomp,as of a wilderness, a mountain, a smoke, a sound of trumpet, and the other like things: but they who were of a higher character, and submissive, and who had risen above mere corporeal imaginations, Yea, for it was removal of punishment, and remission of sins, and “righteousness, and sanctification, and redemption,”and adoption, and an inheritance of Heaven, and a relationship unto the Son of God, which he came declaring unto all; to enemies, to the perverse, to them that were sitting in darkness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then could ever be equal to these good tidings?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: God on earth, man in Heaven; and all became mingled together, angels joined the choirs of men, men had fellowship with the angels, and with the other powers above: and one might see the long war brought to an end, and reconciliation made between God and our nature,the devil brought to shame, demons in flight, death destroyed, Paradise opened, the curse blotted out, sin put out of the way, error driven off, truth returning, the word of godliness everywhere sown, and flourishing in its growth, the polity of those above planted on the earth, those powers in secure intercourse with us, and on earth angels continually haunting, and hope abundant touching things to come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore he hath called the history good tidings, forasmuch as all other things surely are words only without substance; as, for instance, plenty of wealth, greatness of power, kingdoms, and glories, and honors, and whatever other things among men are accounted to be good: but those which are published by the fishermen would be legitimately and properly called good tidings: not only as being sure and immoveable blessings, and beyond our deserts, but also as being given to us with all facility.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not by laboring and sweating, not by fatigue and suffering, but merely as being beloved of God, we received what we have received.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And why can it have been, that when there were so many disciples, two write only from among the apostles, and two from among their followers?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: (For one that was a disciple of Paul, and another of Peter, together with Matthew and John, wrote the Gospels.)

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It was because they did nothing for vainglory, but all things for use.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Was not one evangelist sufficient to tell all?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: One indeed was sufficient; but if there be four that write, not at the same times, nor in the same places, neither after having met together, and conversed one with another, and then they speak all things as it were out of one mouth, this becomes a very great demonstration of the truth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But the contrary,” it may be said, “hath come to pass, for in many places they are convicted of discordance.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, this very thing is a very great evidence of their truth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if they had agreed in all things exactly even to time, and place, and to the very words, none of our enemies would have believed but that they had met together, and had written what they wrote by some human compact; because such entire agreement as this cometh not of simplicity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But now even that discordance which seems to exist in little matters delivers them from all suspicion, and speaks clearly in behalf of the character of the writers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if there be anything touching times or places, which they have related differently, this nothinginjures the truth of what they have said.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And these things too, so far as God shall enable us, we will endeavor, as we proceed, to point out; requiring you, together with what we have mentioned, to observe, that in the chief heads, those which constitute our life and furnish outour doctrine, nowhere is any of them found to have disagreed, no not ever so little.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what are these points?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Such as follow: That God became man, that He wrought miracles, that He was crucified, that He was buried, that He rose again, that He ascended, that He will judge, that He hath given commandments tending to salvation, that He hath brought in a law not contrary to the Old Testament, that He is a Son, that He is only-begotten, that He is a true Son, that He is of the same substance with the Father, and as many things as are like these; for touching these we shall find that there is in them a full agreement.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if amongst the miracles they have not all of them mentioned all, but one these, the other those, let not this trouble thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if on the one hand one had spoken of all, the number of the rest would have been superfluous; and if again all had written fresh things, and different one from another, the proof of their agreement would not have been manifest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause they have both treated of many in common, and each of them hath also received and declared something of his own; that, on the one hand, he might not seem superfluous, and cast on the heapto no purpose; on the other, he might make our test of the truth of their affirmations perfect.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now Luke tells us also the cause wherefore he proceeds to write: “that thou mayest hold,” saith he, “the certainty of the words wherein thou hast been instructed;”that is, that being continually reminded thou mayest hold to the certainty,and abide in certainty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But as to John, he hath himself kept silence touching the cause; yet,(as a traditionsaith, which hath come down to us from the first, even from the Fathers,) neither did he come to write without purpose; but forasmuch as it had been the care of the three to dwell upon the account of the dispensation,and the doctrines of the Godhead were near being left in silence, he, moved by Christ, then and not till then set himself to compose his Gospel.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this is manifest both from the history itself, and from the opening of his Gospel.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he doth not begin like the rest from beneath, but from above, from the same point, at which he was aiming, and it was with a view to this thathe composed the whole book.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And not in the beginning only, but throughout all the Gospel, he is more lofty than the rest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Of Matthew again it is said, that when those who from amongst the Jews had believed came to him, and besought him to leave to them in writing those same things, which he had spoken to them by word, he also composed his Gospel in the language of the Hebrews.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Mark too, in Egypt, is said to have done this self-same thing at the entreaty of the disciples.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause then Matthew, as writing to Hebrews, sought to shew nothing more, than that He was from Abraham, and David; but Luke, as discoursing to all in general, traces up the account higher, going on even to Adam.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the one begins with His generation, because nothing was so soothing to the Jew as to be told that Christ was the offspring of Abraham and David: the other doth not so, but mentions many other things, and then proceeds to the genealogy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But the harmony between them we will establish, both by the whole world, which hath received their statements, and by the very enemies of the truth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For many sects have had birth, since their time, holding opinions opposed to their words; whereof some have received all that they have said, while some have cut off from the rest certain portions of their statements, and so retain them for themselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if there were any hostilityin their statements, neither would the sects, who maintain the contrary part, have received all, but only so much as seemed to harmonize with themselves; nor would those, which have parted off a portion, be utterly refuted by that portion; so that the very fragmentscannot be hid, but declare aloud their connexionwith the whole body.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And like as if thou shouldest take any part from the side of an animal, even in that part thou wouldest find all the things out of which the whole is composed;—nerves and veins, bones, arteries, and blood, and a sample, as one might say, of the whole lump;—so likewise with regard to the Scriptures; in each portion of what is there stated, one may see the connexion with the whole clearly appearing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereas, if they were in discord, neither could this have been pointed out, and the doctrine itself had long since been brought to nought: “for every kingdom,” saith He, “divided against itself shall not stand.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But now even in this shines forth the might of the Spirit, namely, in that it prevailed on these men, engaged as they were in those things which are more necessary and very urgent, to take no hurt at all from these little matters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now, where each one was abiding, when he wrote, it is not right for us to affirm very positively.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But that they are not opposed to each other, this we will endeavor to prove, throughout the whole work.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And thou, in accusing them of disagreement, art doing just the same as if thou wert to insist upon their using the same words and forms of speech.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And I do not yet say, that those likewise who glory greatly in rhetoric and philosophy, having many of them written many books touching the same matters, have not merely expressed themselves differently, but have even spoken in opposition to one another (for it is one thing to speak differently and another to speak at variance); none of these things do I say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Far be it from me to frame our defense from the frenzy of those men, neither am I willing out of falsehood to make recommendations for the truth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But this I would be glad to inquire: how were the differing accounts believed? how did they prevail? how was it that, while saying opposite things, they were admired, were believed, were celebrated everywhere in the world?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet the witnesses of what they said were many, and many too were the adversaries and enemies thereof.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they did not write these things in one corner and bury them, but everywhere, by sea and by land, they unfolded them in the ears of all, and these things were read in the presence of enemies, even as they are now, and none of the things which they said offended any one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And very naturally, for it was a divine power that pervaded all, and made it to prosper with all men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if it had not been so, how could the publican, and the fisherman, and the unlearned, have attained to such philosophy?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For things, which they that are without have never been able to imagine, no not in a dream, are by these men with great certainty both published and made convincing, and not in their lives only, but even after death: neither to two men, nor twenty men, nor an hundred, nor a thousand, nor ten thousand, but to cities, nations, and people, both to land and sea, in the land both of Greeks and barbarians, both inhabited and desert; and all concerning things far beyond our nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For leaving the earth, all their discourse is concerning the things in heaven, while they bring in unto us another principle of life, another manner of living: both wealth and poverty, freedom and slavery, life and death, our world and our polity, all changed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not like Plato, who composed that ridiculous Republic,or Zeno, or if there be any one else that hath written a polity, or hath framed laws.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed, touching all these, it hath been made manifest by themselves, that an evil spirit, and some cruel demon at war with our race, a foe to modesty, and an enemy to good order, oversetting all things, hath made his voice be heard in their soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When, for example, they make their women common to all, and stripping virgins naked in the Palæstra, bring them into the gaze of men; and when they establish secret marriages, mingling all things together and confounding them, and overturning the limits of nature, what else is there to say?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that these their sayings are all inventions of devils, and contrary to nature, even nature herself would testify, not tolerating what we have mentioned; and this, though they write not amidst persecutions, nor dangers, nor fightings, but in all security and freedom, and deck it out with many ornaments from many sources.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these doctrines of the fishermen, chased as they were, scourged and in jeopardy, both learned and unlearned, both bond and free, both kings and private soldiers, both barbarians and Greeks, have received with all good will.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And thou canst not say, that it was because these things were trifling and low, that they were easily to be received by all men: nay, for these doctrines are far higher than those.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as to virginity, they never imagined even the name thereof so much as in a dream, nor yet of voluntary poverty, nor of fasting, nor of any other of those things that are high.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But they that are of our part not only exterminate lust, they chastise not only the act, but even an unchaste look, and insulting language, and disorderly laughter, and dress, and gait, and clamor, and they carry on their exactness even to the smallest things, and have filled the whole earth with the plant of virginity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And touching God too, and the things in heaven, they persuade men to be wisewith such knowledge as no one of those hath at any time been able so much as to conceive in his mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For how could they, who made for gods images of beasts, and of monsters that crawl on the earth, and of other things still more vile?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet these high doctrines were both accepted and believed, and they flourish every day and increase; but the others have passed away, and perished, having disappeared more easily than spiders’ webs.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And very naturally, for they were demons that published these things; wherefore besides their uncleanness, their obscurity is great, and the labor they require greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what could be more ridiculous than that “republic,”in which, besides what I have mentioned, the philosopher, when he hath spent lines without number, that he may be able to shew what justice is, hath over and above this prolixity filled his discourse with much indistinctness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This, even if it did contain anything profitable, must needs be very useless for the life of man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if the husbandman and the smith, the builder and the pilot, and every one who subsists by the labor of his hands, is to leave his trade, and his honest toils, and is to spend such and such a number of years in order to learn what justice is; before he has learnt he will often times be absolutely destroyed by hunger, and perish because of this justice, not having learnt anything else useful to be known, and having ended his life by a cruel death.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But our lessons are not such; rather Christ hath taughtus what is just, and what is seemly, and what is expedient, and all virtue in general, comprising it in few and plain words: at one time saying that, “on two commandments hang the Law and the Prophets;”that is to say, on the love of God and on the love of our neighbor: at another time, “Whatsoever ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them; for this is the Law and the Prophets.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And these things even to a laborer, and to a servant, and to a widow woman, and to a very child, and to him that appeareth to be exceedingly slow of understanding, are all plain to comprehend and easy to learn.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the lessons of the truth are like this; and the actual result bears witness thereto.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: All at least have learned what things they are to do, and not learned only, but been emulous also of them; and not in the cities alone nor in the midst of the market places, but also in the summits of the mountains.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for there wilt thou see true wisdomabounding, and choirs of angels shining forth in a human body, and the commonwealthof Heaven manifested here on earth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For a commonwealthdid these fishermen too write for us, not with commands that it should be embraced from childhood, like those others, nor making it a law that the virtuous man must be so many years old, but addressing their discourse generally to every age.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For those lessons are children’s toys, but these are the truth of things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And as a place for this their commonwealththey have assigned Heaven, and God they have brought in as the framer thereof, and as lawgiver of the statutes there set; as indeed was their duty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the rewards in their commonwealthare not leaves of bay nor olive, nor an allowance of meat in the public hall, nor statues of brass, these cold and ordinary things, but a life which hath no end, and to become children of God, to join the angels’ choir, and to stand by the royal throne, and to be always with Christ.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the popular guides of this commonwealthare publicans, and fishermen, and tent-makers, not such as have lived for a short time, but such as are now living for ever.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore even after their death they may possibly do the greatest good to the governed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This republicis at war not with men, but with devils, and those incorporeal powers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also their captain is no one of men, nor of angels, but God Himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the armor too of these warriors suits the nature of the warfare, for it is not formed of hides and steel, but of truth and of righteousness, and faith, and all true love of wisdom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since then the aforesaid republicis both the subject on which this book was written, and it is now proposed for us to speak thereof, let us give careful heed to Matthew, discoursing plainly concerning this: for what he saith is not his own, but all Christ’s, who hath made the laws of this city.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us give heed, I say, that we may be capable of enrolment therein, and of shining forth among those that have already become citizens thereof, and are awaiting those incorruptible crowns.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To many, however, this discourse seems to be easy, while the prophetic writings are difficult.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But this again is the view of men who know not the depth of the thoughts laid up therein.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore I entreat you to follow us with much diligence, so as to enter into the very ocean of the things written, with Christ for our guide at this our entering in.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But in order that the word may be the more easy to learn, we pray and entreat you, as we have done also with respect to the other Scriptures, to take up beforehand that portion of the Scripture which we may be going to explain, that your reading may prepare the way for your understanding (as also was the case with the eunuch), and so may greatly facilitate our task.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this becausethe questions are many and frequent.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See, for instance, at once in the beginning of his Gospel, how many difficulties might be raised one after the other.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As first, wherefore the genealogy of Joseph is traced, who was not father of Christ.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Secondly, whence may it be made manifest that He derives His origin from David, while the forefathers of Mary, who bare Him, are not known, for the Virgin’s genealogy is not traced?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thirdly, on what account Joseph’s genealogy is traced, when he had nothing to do with the birth; while with regard to the Virgin, who was the very mother, it is not shown of what fathers, or grandfathers, or ancestors, she is sprung.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And along with these things, this is also worth inquiry, wherefore it can be, that, when tracing the genealogy through the men, he hath mentioned women also; and why since he determined upon doing this, he yet did not mention them all, but passing over the more eminent, such as Sarah, Rebecca, and as many as are like them, he hath brought forward only them that are famed for some bad thing; as, for instance, if any was a harlot, or an adulteress, or a mother by an unlawful marriage, if any was a stranger or barbarian.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he hath made mention of the wife of Uriah, and of Thamar, and of Rahab, and of Ruth, of whom one was of a strange race, another an harlot, another was defiled by her near kinsman, and with him not in the form of marriage, but by a stolen intercourse, when she had put on herself the mask of an harlot; and touching the wife of Uriah no one is ignorant, by reason of the notoriety of the crime.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet the evangelist hath passed by all the rest, and inserted in the genealogy these alone.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereas, if women were to be mentioned, all ought to be so; if not all but some, then those famed in the way of virtue, not for evil deeds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See you how much care is required of us straightway in the first beginning? and yet the beginning seems to be plainer than the rest; to many perhaps even superfluous, as being a mere numbering of names.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After this, another point again is worth inquiry; wherefore he hath omitted three kings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if, because they were exceeding ungodly, he therefore passed by their names in silence, neither should he have mentioned the others, that were like them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this againis another question; why, after having spoken of fourteen generations, he hath not in the third division maintained the number.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And wherefore Luke hath made mention of other names, and not only not all of them the same, but also many more of them, while Matthew hath both fewer and different, though he too hath ended with Joseph, with whom Luke likewise concluded.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Ye see how much wakeful attention is needed on our part, not only for explanation, but even that we may learn what things we have to explain.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither is this a little matter, to be able to find out the difficulties; there being also this other hard point, how Elizabeth, who was of the Levitical tribe, was kinswoman to Mary.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But that we may not overload your memory, by stringing many things together, here let us stay our discourse for a time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is enough for you in order that ye be thoroughly roused, that you learnthe questions only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if ye long fortheir solution also, this again depends on yourselves, before we speak.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if I see you thoroughly awakened, and longing to learn, I will endeavor to add the solution also; but if gaping and not attending, I will conceal both the difficulties, and their solution, in obedience to a divine law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, saith He, “Give not the holy things to the dogs, neither cast ye your pearls before swine, lest they trample them under their feet.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But who is he that tramples them under foot?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He that doth not account these things precious, and venerable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And who, it may be asked, is so wretched as not to esteem these things venerable, and more precious than all?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He who doth not bestow on them so much leisure as on the harlot women in the theatres of Satan.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For there the multitude pass the whole day, and give up not a few of their domestic concerns for the sake of this unseasonable employment, and they retain with exactness whatever they have heard, and this though it be to the injury of their souls, that they keep it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But here, where God is speaking, they will not bear to tarry even a little time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore, let me warn you, we have nothing in common with Heaven, but our citizenshipgoes no further than words.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet because of this, God hath threatened even hell, not in order to cast us therein, but that He might persuade us to flee this grievous tyranny.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But we do the opposite, and run each day the way that leads thither, and while God is commanding us not only to hear, but also to do what He saith, we do not submit so much as to hearken.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When then, I pray thee, are we to do what is commanded, and to put our hand to the works, if we do not endure so much as to hear the words that relate to them, but are impatient and restless about the time we stay here, although it be exceedingly short?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And besides, when we are talking of indifferent matters, if we see those that are in company do not attend, we call what they do an insult; but do we consider that we are provoking God, if, while He is discoursing of such things as these, we despise what is said, and look another way?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, he that is grown old, and hath travelled over much country, reports to us with all exactness the number of stadia, and the situations of cities, their plans, and their harbors and markets; but we ourselves know not even how far we are from the city that is in Heaven.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For surely we should have endeavored to shorten the space, had we known the distance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That city being not only as far from us as Heaven is from the earth, but even much farther, if we be negligent; like as, on the other hand, if we do our best,even in one instant we shall come to the gates thereof.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not by local space, but by moral disposition, are these distances defined.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But thou knowest exactly the affairs of the world, as well new as old, and such too as are quite ancient; thou canst number the princes under whom thou hast served in time past, and the ruler of the games, and them that gained the prize, and the leaders of armies, matters that are of no concern to thee; but who hath become ruler in this city, the first or the second or the third, and for how long, each of them; and what each hath accomplished, and brought to pass, thou hast not imagined even as in a dream.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the laws that are set in this city thou wilt not endure to hear, nor attend to them, even when others tell thee of them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then, I pray thee, dost thou expect to obtain the blessings that are promised, when thou dost not even attend to what is said?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But though never before, now, at any rate, let us do this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for weare on the point of entering into a city (if God permit) of gold, and more precious than any gold.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then mark her foundations, her gates consisting of sapphires and pearls; for indeed we have in Matthew an excellent guide.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For through his gate we shall now enter in, and much diligence is required on our part.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For should He see any one not attentive, He casts him out of the city.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yes, for the city is most kingly and glorious; not as the cities with us, divided into a market-place, and the royal courts; for there all is the court of the King.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us open therefore the gates of our mind, let us open our ears, and with great trembling, when on the point of setting foot on the threshold, let us worship the King that is therein.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed the first approach hath power straightway to confound the beholder.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the present we find the gates closed; but when we see them thrown open (for this is the solution of the difficulties), then we shall perceive the greatness of the splendor within.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For there also, leading thee with the eyes of the Spirit, is one who offers to show thee all, even this Publican; where the King sitteth, and who of His host stand by Him; where are the angels, where the archangels; and what place is set apart for the new citizens in this city, and what kind of way it is that leads thither, and what manner of portion they have received, who first were citizens therein, and those next after them, and such as followed these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how many are the orders of these tribes, how many those of the senate, how many the distinctions of dignity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not therefore with noise or tumult enter in, but with a mystical silence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if in a theatre, when a great silence hath been made, then the letters of the king are read, much more in this city must all be composed, and stand with soul and ear erect.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is not the letters of any earthly master, but of the Lord of angels, which are on the point of being read.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If we would order ourselves on this wise, the grace itself of the Spirit will lead us in great perfection, and we shall arrive at the very royal throne, and attain to all the good things, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and might, together with the Father and the Holy Ghost, now and always, even for ever and ever.
